did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning, specifically 3D-discriminant analysis (3D-PCA-QDA) combined with ATR-FTIR spectroscopy, to identify and classify basal cell carcinoma (BCC) skin cancer versus healthy skin, achieving an accuracy of 99%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results using ATR-FTIR spectroscopy and 3D-discriminant analysis to identify basal cell carcinoma. It details specific methods (sample preparation, spectral acquisition, preprocessing), a dataset of 44 samples, and reports new performance metrics (99% accuracy). Although published in conference proceedings (OMN/SBFoton), it contains original research data and analysis rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Identification of basal cell carcinoma skin cancer using FTIR and Machine learning
journal: 2023 International Conference on Optical MEMS and Nanophotonics (OMN) and SBFoton International Optics and Photonics Conference (SBFoton IOPC)
brief_summary: This study aimed to identify basal cell carcinoma (BCC) skin cancer versus healthy skin using Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier Transform Infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy combined with machine learning. The dataset consisted of 44 paraffin-embedded samples, including 29 BCC tumor tissues and 15 healthy skin specimens. Spectral data were pre-processed using Savitzky-Golay filtering, baseline correction, vector normalization, and extended multiplicative scatter correction (EMSC) within the fingerprint region of 1800–900 cm⁻¹. The computational modeling employed a 3D-discriminant analysis algorithm (3D-PCA-QDA) with data balanced via SMOTE-TOMEK and validated using a Leave One Out (LOO) method. The model achieved an exceptional accuracy of 99%, as determined by the area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. The results indicate that 3D-discriminant algorithms combined with ATR-FTIR spectroscopy offer high precision for differentiating BCC from healthy tissues.

lesion_types_studied.details: 29 samples of BCC tumor tissue were studied as the primary disease entity.
15 samples of healthy skin were used as the control/comparator group.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) tumor tissue and healthy skin
all_task_types.details: The study uses 3D-discriminant analysis (3D-PCA-QDA) to classify spectral data into two discrete diagnostic categories: BCC skin cancer versus healthy skin. The model achieved an accuracy of 99% based on the area under the ROC curve (AUC). The dataset consisted of 44 samples (29 BCC, 15 healthy).

datasets_used.name: Paraffin-embedded BCC and Healthy Skin Spectral Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom experimental dataset consisting of 44 paraffin-embedded tissue samples analyzed using ATR-FTIR spectroscopy. The samples were obtained from the authors' institutions (IPEN, UNLP). Spectral data were recorded between 4000–400 cm⁻¹ with a resolution of 4 cm⁻¹ and 100 scans per spectrum, focusing on the fingerprint region of 1800–900 cm⁻¹.

use.training: Used for training the 3D-PCA-QDA model. The dataset was split into 80% training (35 samples) and 20% testing (9 samples). SMOTE-TOMEK technique was applied to balance the unbalanced data for training.
use.validation: Leave One Out (LOO) method was used to confirm model validation.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance (20% of the dataset, 9 samples).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To confirm the validation of the 3D-PCA-QDA model.
To balance the unbalanced dataset (transforming unbalanced data into balanced data) for training the model.
To evaluate model performance and calculate accuracy using the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
To classify spectral data and differentiate between BCC tumor tissue and healthy skin using discriminant functions based on covariance matrices.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy (99%) derived from the area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and validated via Leave One Out (LOO) cross-validation.

Paraffin-embedded BCC and Healthy Skin S.metric: Accuracy (Area Under the Curve of ROC)
Paraffin-embedded BCC and Healthy Skin S.notes: The model achieved an accuracy of up to 99% using the area under the receiver operating characteristics (ROC) curve. The dataset consisted of 44 samples (29 BCC tumor tissue, 15 healthy skin). Data was split into 80% training (35 samples) and 20% testing (9 samples). SMOTE-TOMEK was used to balance the training data. Validation was confirmed using the Leave One Out (LOO) method. The discriminant function showed optimal separation between normal and BCC samples.

architecture_used.input_size: Fingerprint region of 1800–900 cm⁻¹ with a step of 4 cm⁻¹

architecture_details.algorithm_type: 3D-principal component analysis (3D-PCA) combined with Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA)
architecture_details.decomposition_method: Nonlinear iterative partial least squares (NIPLAS) algorithm
architecture_details.discriminant_function: Qij = (xi − xj)T Cj−1 (xi − xj) + loge |Cj| − 2loge πj
architecture_details.covariance_matrices: Pooled covariance matrix (Cpooled) and class variance-covariance matrix (Cj)

training_details.data_balancing: SMOTE-TOMEK technique applied to transform unbalanced data into balanced data

classification_or_output_head.evaluation_metric: Area Under the Curve (AUC) of the Receiver Operating Characteristics (ROC) curve

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: nonlinear iterative partial least squares (NIPLAS) algorithm
preprocessing_techniques: Extended Multiplicative Scatter Correction (EMSC) for dispersion correction
Selection of fingerprint region (1800–900 cm⁻¹) with a step of 4 cm⁻¹

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to balance the unbalanced dataset (transforming unbalanced data into balanced data) for training the model. Used on the training set (80% of the dataset) to achieve higher precision.

conclusions: The study concludes that combining ATR-FTIR spectroscopy with 3D-discriminant analysis (3D-PCA-QDA) is an effective computational approach for identifying basal cell carcinoma (BCC) skin cancer versus healthy skin.
The proposed method achieved an exceptional accuracy of up to 99%, as determined by the area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve.
The discriminant function demonstrated optimal separation between normal and BCC samples, indicating high precision in differentiating the two tissue types.

The results indicate the potential performance of 3D-discriminant algorithms in comparison with unfolded algorithms, evidencing outstanding performance for identifying cancer disease using ATR-FTIR data.
The study suggests that ATR-FTIR spectroscopy combined with machine learning could lead to faster and more accurate BCC diagnosis, thereby improving clinical outcomes.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset of only 44 paraffin-embedded samples (29 BCC, 15 healthy), which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset is highly imbalanced (29 BCC vs. 15 healthy), requiring the use of SMOTE-TOMEK for data balancing, which may introduce synthetic bias.
Model validation was performed using Leave One Out (LOO) cross-validation on the entire dataset, which is prone to overfitting and optimistic bias, especially with such a small sample size.
There is no independent external validation cohort; the model was tested on the same small dataset used for training and validation.
The paper does not report standard deviation, confidence intervals, or p-values for the reported 99% accuracy, limiting the assessment of statistical significance.
The use of paraffin-embedded tissues may introduce artifacts from the embedding process that differ from fresh or frozen clinical samples, affecting clinical translatability.
The study is published in conference proceedings rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may indicate less rigorous scrutiny compared to full journal publications.
